notes.note: The "loyalists" (boni viri, the "good men") are the optimates, the conservative republican camp; M. Brutus has reported that the young Quintus claims to be coming over to their side, which Cicero treats with sardonic disbelief.
The temple of Athena on the Athenian Acropolis; Atticus had owned or admired some artistic piece (philotechnema) there depicting the tyrant-slayers, which Cicero recalls having seen.

C. Servilius Ahala (who killed the would-be tyrant Sp. Maelius in 439 BC) and L. Iunius Brutus (who expelled the kings and founded the Republic in 509 BC), the canonical Roman tyrannicides. They are M. Brutus's claimed ancestors, not the living Brutus, and the depiction makes the wry point about resisting one-man rule.
A scornful periphrasis, most likely for Caesar, whose autocracy Cicero blames for the ruin of the Republic; the identification is inferred from context, as Cicero leaves the man unnamed.

The Latin "ad Saxa" ("to the Rocks") appears to name a place to which Quintus the father is travelling, perhaps the Saxa Rubra on the Via Flaminia north of Rome, but the reference is corrupt and uncertain. No secure entity can be assigned.
